ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Paneer Chatpata Masala; closest_parent is cottage cheese food product (FOODON_03302050) as paneer is a fresh Indian cottage cheese.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Cheese dishes) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for paneer masala preparations exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 identifies paneer as a substance; no specific concept for Paneer Chatpata Masala dish exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Paneer Chatpata Masala is approximately 250 g, consisting of paneer cubes in a spiced masala sauce, usually served alongside naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer (cottage cheese, Indian) and Paneer curry dish — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Paneer Chatpata Masala is not listed as a single entry in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition): paneer (raw) nutrient profile combined with a standard North Indian masala curry preparation adjustment. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate are weighted estimates reflecting approximately 40% paneer by weight with tomato-onion-spice masala base and cooking oil. Secondary cross-reference: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cottage cheese (full fat) and Indian curry sauce components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal); GI values cross-referenced with University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Paneer Chatpata Masala. GI estimated at 27 based on the GI of paneer (fresh cheese, GI ~27, low GI food per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care tables). The masala sauce components (tomatoes, onions, spices) contribute negligible glycemic load. Carbohydrate content per serving (~18.5 g from 250 g portion) yields GL = (27 × 18.5) / 100 ≈ 5.0. gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; restaurant preparations may contain additional salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size as a 250 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 775 mg sodium, representing about one-third of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; request reduced-salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~27) driven by high-protein, high-fat paneer base with minimal refined carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paneer Chatpata Masala has a low GI of approximately 27 and a glycemic load of approximately 5 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or plain naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content approximately 6.1 g per 100 g from full-fat paneer and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content of approximately 15 g per 250 g serving warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing low-fat paneer preparations and limiting portion frequency to 1–2 times per week is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Chatpata Masala is a popular North Indian vegetarian dish featuring cubes of paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) cooked in a bold, spicy, and tangy masala sauce. The name 'chatpata' in Hindi refers to a sharp, piquant, mouth-watering flavour profile achieved through the combination of chaat masala, amchur (dried mango powder), green chillies, and aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, and turmeric. The dish is prepared by sautéing onions, garlic, and ginger in oil, adding tomatoes and whole or ground spices to form a thick masala base, then folding in paneer cubes and finishing with fresh coriander. The texture is soft and creamy from the paneer against a thick, slightly chunky sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of protein (approximately 9.8 g per 100 g) and calcium (approximately 190 mg per 100 g) from the paneer, with moderate fat content (approximately 11.2 g per 100 g) and a low glycemic index of approximately 27, making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium content is moderate at approximately 310 mg per 100 g but may be higher in restaurant preparations. The dish is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian. It is commonly served with naan, roti, or basmati rice and is a staple on Indian restaurant menus in Singapore and globally. Clinically, it is a reasonable protein source for vegetarians managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI, but saturated fat and sodium should be monitored for cardiovascular and hypertension management.
common_user_queries: Is paneer masala good for diabetics?
Is Paneer Chatpata Masala gluten free?
What does chatpata mean in Indian food?

confidence_reason: Paneer Chatpata Masala is not listed as a single standardised entry in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN paneer (raw) data and standard North Indian curry preparation estimates. GI is estimated by analogy from paneer GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary ingredient (paneer) has well-established nutrient data from ICMR-NIN and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on ICMR-NIN paneer data and standard masala curry composition; exact values will vary by restaurant preparation. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for paneer or standard Indian curry preparations. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for paneer or Indian curry dishes. 5. GI of 27 is an analogous estimate based on paneer GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Paneer Chatpata Masala was found in approved sources. 6. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy data for paneer and cooking oil components. 7. The dish is served at Kinara - Boat Quay on field survey data at SGD 14.90. 8. This is an Indian cuisine dish served in Singapore; multilingual names are provided in Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean multilingual naming rules.
